text: So this is another in our series of challenging or fissureless robotic lobectomies. This one's going to focus on a right middle lobectomy. I think the challenge here is figuring out where the minor fissure is. This patient actually had a tumor that was abutting the minor fissure, and we wanted to make sure we had a good margin, but often the minor fissure is incomplete, and this will address the challenges of figuring out where the fissure is when you don't see a fissure. And I think this is similar to when you start tackling segmentectomies and where the intersegmental plane is, and obviously there's tricks for combating that with intravenous ICG, but this one we're going to focus on figuring out exactly how to find where the fissure is when the fissure is incomplete.

So here's the CT scan, the sagittal cuts, and you'll see the The tumor in the right middle lobe, it turned out to be an adenocarcinoma. It's abutting the minor fissure. The patient did have some increased metabolic activity on the PET scan in the hyaluronodes, and I did an EBUS on him beforehand, and those nodes were all, the central mediastinal nodes were all negative. He ended up having a couple peribronchial nodes that were positive, so he had N-I disease, but there's the tumors.

The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for a robotic right middle lobectomy, using two 12mm and two 8mm ports. The 8mm camera port is positioned first, typically at the junction between the sternum and xiphoid, around the seventh intercostal space. The placement is adjusted for the middle lobe bronchus or artery, considering the tight fit in smaller patients due to rib proximity.

The surgeon positions the medial staple port one intercostal space higher than the camera port, typically between the sixth and seventh ribs. The twelve millimeter air seal port is triangulated between the medial staple port and the camera port. The assistant port is placed as low as possible at the junction between the diaphragm and the chest, approximately ten to twelve centimeters from the other ports, allowing room for maneuvering. The lateral ports are spaced six to eight centimeters apart.

The surgeon begins the lobectomy by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament. The fourth robotic arm is used to elevate the diaphragm surface of the right lower lobe, creating tension on the ligament. The surgeon harvests the station 9R lymph nodes and isolates the inferior pulmonary vein, carefully avoiding direct manipulation of the lower lobe with the instruments.

The surgeon dissects in the subcarinal space to retrieve station seven lymph nodes. The assistant applies pressure on the basilar segments while the surgeon uses a fourth arm to push on the superior segments, enhancing exposure. Bleeding is managed with a sponge roll to absorb excess blood.

The surgeon retrieves subcarinal lymph node tissue using fingertips of gloves to prevent dropping material, packing the area with Fibrillar. The upper lobe is reflected inferiorly to access paratracheal lymph nodes, dissecting nodal tissue between the vena cava, bronchus, and azygos vein down to the pericardium.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to hold the upper lobe down while employing a zero-degree camera positioned in the seventh intercostal space, providing a clear view of the surgical field and allowing the assistant more room to maneuver.

The surgeon reflects the lung posteriorly to expose the medial hilum, isolating the superior pulmonary vein and identifying the middle vein branch. A second sponge is used for sweeping, and the inferior pulmonary vein is visible at the bottom of the screen. The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the middle vein with a vessel loop, avoiding division to prevent bleeding and congestion.

The surgeon attempts to expose the pulmonary artery within the fissure, noting the yellow hue of the artery despite the thick fissure. A station XI lymph node is dissected, located medial to the interlobar pulmonary artery. The surgeon uses the fourth arm to push the middle lobe upward, employing a sponge roll for cushioning and blunt dissection, while another sponge roll is used to absorb blood from minor bleeding.

The surgeon dissects the middle lobe artery, aiming to expose the hilar structures by dividing the medial aspect of the major fissure. The minor fissure and the medial aspect of the major fissure are identified as key areas for division. Bronchiolymph nodes are dissected off the middle lobe bronchus to enhance exposure.

The surgeon examines the medial aspect of the fissure from below, exposing it next to the pulmonary artery. Using a longer, curved blunt instrument, the surgeon connects the exposed areas of the fissure. A Rob Nell catheter is positioned to protect the pulmonary artery, allowing the stapler's anvil to safely divide the medial aspect of the major fissure with a blue load.

The surgeon divides the fissure to expose the middle lobe bronchus and artery. The fourth arm holds the middle lobe up and away, providing a clear view of the middle lobe vein, bronchus, and artery. The surgeon decides to take the middle lobe vein first, dividing the venous branches separately to ensure proper drainage. The middle lobe bronchus is visible coursing underneath the interlobar pulmonary artery, and the second venous branch draining the middle lobe is divided.

The surgeon fires staples from the medial staple port and begins dissecting the medial aspect of the middle lobe bronchus. A pair of bronchial lymph nodes is visible, with one appearing vascular, indicating possible involvement in N-one disease.

The surgeon is positioned underneath the middle lobe bronchus, preparing to divide it using a green load stapler. The Robnel catheter is visible, protecting the middle lobe artery on the opposite side. The peribronchial lymph node appears inflamed and vascular. The surgeon then proceeds to dissect and remove the remaining lymph node tissue. The middle lobe artery is exposed, and a gray load stapler is used to divide it, with all staple fires executed from the medial port.

The surgeon examines the minor fissure, noting the tumor's encroachment and adjusts the approach to ensure a margin by angling towards the upper lobe. A long SureForm stapler is used to create a clean staple line, with some staples fired from the lateral port.

The surgeon divides connective tissue tethering the lobe to the superior pulmonary vein, identifying a small venous branch. Medium metal clips are applied to secure the branch before using hot scissors for further dissection. The surgeon assesses the intersegmental plane, ensuring vascular and bronchial structures are positioned outside the staple line, and prepares to use a gray load to remove the segmental vein branch.

The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler from the posterior port to connect the remaining lung tissue, ensuring the staple line is below the divided hilar structures and maintains a margin from the tumor. The middle lobe is reflected in various directions to optimize stapler angle, and a blue load is used to detach the middle lobe, which is then placed in an anchor bag for removal.
